title: How Cursor Works Under The Hood, Understands Your Code (and Why It Feels Like Magic)
author: Saket Anand
text:
How Cursor Works Under The Hood, Really Understands Your Code (and Why It Feels Like Magic)
If you’ve used Cursor for more than a few days, you’ve probably felt it.
- You ask “Where is auth handled?”
→ Cursor jumps straight to the right files. - You ask “Show all usages of OrdersService”
→ It understands intent, not just text.
This isn’t autocomplete.
It isn’t just grep.
It’s Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), built specifically for code.
- RAG in one line:
Retrieve relevant chunks → add them to the prompt → generate the answer.
The model never sees the whole repo, only what matters.
The Problem Cursor Is Solving
- Large repositories do not fit into an AI’s context window.
- Even the biggest models can’t read:
• an entire monorepo
• all services
• configs
• docs - Trying to dump everything into context makes answers worse:
• attention spreads thin
• important details get lost in the middle
• responses become vague or incorrect
Cursor avoids this entirely.
- Instead of asking “How do I show everything?”
- It asks:
“What small pieces of this codebase matter right now?”
Step 1: Watching Your Code Without Uploading It
When you open a workspace, Cursor does not upload your codebase.
What it does instead:
- Builds a Merkle tree locally
- Every file gets a hash
- Every folder hash depends on its children
- Change one file → only one branch changes
This lets Cursor:
- detect exactly what changed
- sync only those changes
- avoid reprocessing the whole repo
Practical impact:
- In a 50,000-file repo, editing one file might sync only a few KB
- Your code stays local
- Sync stays fast
Step 2: Chunking Code the Right Way
Cursor does not split code by token count.
Instead:
- Uses tree-sitter, a real parser
- Understands syntax and structure
Chunks are:
- one function
- one class
- one logical block
Why this matters:
- Embeddings work better on meaningful units
- Functions describe intent
- Random text slices don’t
Edge cases:
- Very large or complex files may be skipped or partially indexed
- Tree-sitter can struggle with very large files (~32KB in some environments), so those get different handling
Step 3: Turning Code Into Meaning (Embeddings)
Each chunk becomes a vector embedding — numbers that represent meaning.
Important detail:
- This is not a generic text embedding
- Cursor trains its own code-specific embedding model
How it’s trained:
- Uses real agent sessions
- What people searched
- What files they opened
- What actually helped solve the task
- An LLM ranks “what would have been most helpful”
- The embedding model learns from those rankings
Result:
- Cursor learns how developers actually explore code
- Not just theoretical similarity
Efficiency:
- If a chunk hasn’t changed, the embedding is reused
- No wasted compute
Step 4: Privacy by Design
Before anything leaves your machine:
- File paths are obfuscated
- Directory structure is preserved, names are hidden
- Source code is never stored on Cursor servers
What is stored:
- embeddings
- metadata (encrypted paths, line ranges)
Even if someone accessed the vector DB:
- They would not see your code
Step 5: Where the Vectors Live (Turbopuffer)
Cursor stores embeddings in Turbopuffer, a high-throughput vector database.
Key ideas:
- Each (user_id, codebase) has its own namespace
- Active namespaces:
- live in RAM / NVMe
- fast (single-digit ms) - Inactive namespaces:
- move to object storage
- cheap, slightly slower when reactivated
Result:
- Your current repo feels instant
- Old repos don’t waste memory
Index reuse with simhash
When two people in the same org open the same repo:
- Cursor computes a simhash from the Merkle tree
- Finds a teammate’s existing index
- Reuses embeddings instead of recomputing
Numbers that matter:
- Codebases in the same org are ~92% similar
- Time-to-first-query on large repos:
• from 4+ hours
• down to ~21 seconds
Privacy preserved:
- Client uploads its Merkle tree
- Server only returns results for code you actually have
- No cross-user leakage
Summary:
- Same code → same embeddings
- Different users → isolated namespaces
Step 6: When You Ask a Question
When you type:
“Where is user authentication handled?”
Cursor does all of this:
- Converts your question into an embedding
- Searches for similar code chunks
- Runs grep for exact matches
- Pulls in:
• open files
• semantic chunks
• symbolic connections
• lint
• git history
• docs
• typing history
Total candidate context:
- Up to ~500k tokens
Hybrid search (why it feels precise)
Cursor uses both:
- Semantic search
for intent (“where is auth handled?”) - Grep
for exact matches (“OrdersService”)
The agent:
- chooses which to use
- often combines them
This hybrid approach is a big reason Cursor feels so accurate.
Step 7: The Reranker (The Real Brain)
Cursor can’t send 500k tokens to an LLM.
So it uses a custom reranker:
- Scores all candidate context
- Filters it down to the most relevant ~8k tokens
- Selects, not compresses
Important:
- Context selection is still an open research problem
- Cursor keeps improving this layer
Step 8: Final Answer Generation
Only now does the LLM step in.
What it sees:
- just the right parts of your codebase
- not too much
- not too little
That’s why Cursor:
- explains existing code well
- answers “how does this work?” clearly
- scales to very large repositories
Why You Can’t “Doc the Whole Repo” in One Shot
Common request:
- “Generate documentation for the entire repo”
Why this fails:
- Context windows are finite
- Attention weakens as context grows
- Content in the middle gets ignored (“lost in the middle”)
Cursor avoids this by design:
- No single giant context
- Retrieval happens per question, per moment
That’s why it works.
Known Limitations
Cursor isn’t magic.
Things to know:
- Index refresh is about 5 minutes
- Recent edits may not be searchable yet
- Unsaved buffer edits aren’t indexed
→ Pro tip: save before asking, or use @ filename - Query latency depends on cache:
• warm: ~8–10ms
• cold: ~500–600ms - Generic queries can surface generic utility code
- Large or auto-generated files may be skipped
- Retrieval quality depends heavily on .cursorignore
Still, Cursor’s A/B tests show:
- ~12.5% higher accuracy on code questions
- ~2.6% better retention on large codebases (1,000+ files)
- ~2.2% fewer dissatisfied follow-ups
The gains are modest overall — but much larger where it matters: big repos.
Why This Feels So Good for Vibe Coding
Cursor doesn’t just search code.
It matches how developers think:
- jump between files
- follow call chains
- skim instead of reading everything
- ask fuzzy questions
It’s less like grep.
More like a very fast teammate who’s already explored the repo.
Learn to Use Cursor Better for Vibe Coding
In the next post, I’ll focus less on how Cursor works and more on how to use it well: prompts that work, common mistakes, and workflows that actually speed you up.
Follow if that sounds useful.
About Me: https://www.saketanand.me/
